text.title: AWS VPC Connectivity Patterns
text.summary: VPC Peering vs Transit Gateway decision, VPC Lattice, IPAM, and connectivity checklist.

section.title: VPC Peering vs Transit Gateway

rule.statement: Use VPC Peering for 2-3 VPCs in the same region; use Transit Gateway for 4+ VPCs, cross-region connectivity, or hybrid on-premises connectivity via Direct Connect or VPN.
rule.rationale: VPC Peering is simpler and has no per-hour attachment cost — ideal for small deployments. Transit Gateway uses a hub-and-spoke model that scales to thousands of attachments without N×(N-1)/2 peering connections.

rule.statement: Configure Transit Gateway route tables segmented by environment (prod/non-prod isolation); disable DefaultRouteTableAssociation and DefaultRouteTablePropagation; use blackhole routes for deprecated networks.
rule.rationale: Default route tables associate all attachments together, creating unintended connectivity between production and development VPCs. Explicit route table assignment enforces environment isolation.

section.note: Decision quick reference: 2-3 VPCs same region → VPC Peering, 4+ VPCs or cross-region → Transit Gateway, hybrid on-prem → Transit Gateway + Direct Connect/VPN, service-to-service mesh → VPC Lattice.

rule.statement: Use VPC Lattice for application-layer service-to-service connectivity across VPCs and accounts; prefer over VPC Peering when services need built-in auth, routing, and observability.
rule.rationale: VPC Lattice provides per-service access controls, request-level observability, and HTTP/gRPC routing without managing security groups across peered VPCs. It replaces complex PrivateLink setups for internal service meshes.

rule.statement: For VPC Peering: enable DNS resolution across peers, reference peer VPC security groups by ID, configure specific routes (not full CIDR), verify no transitive routing assumptions.
rule.rationale: VPC Peering is not transitive — VPC A peered to VPC B and B peered to VPC C does not create A-to-C connectivity. Teams frequently discover this misconfiguration in production when services cannot communicate.

rule.statement: For all multi-VPC deployments: use IPAM for central IP planning and overlap detection; document all CIDR allocations; reserve expansion ranges in each VPC.
rule.rationale: CIDR overlaps block all peering and TGW attachment options. IPAM provides real-time overlap detection and prevents allocation conflicts in multi-account Organizations.

antipattern.description: Assuming VPC Peering is transitive for multi-hop connectivity.
antipattern.reason: VPC Peering is point-to-point only. Traffic cannot pass through a peered VPC to reach a third VPC. Transit Gateway or VPC Lattice is required for hub-and-spoke topologies.
